title: The Uncapped Ledger
subtitle: How Malaysia's Real Fiscal Exposure Hides Outside the Number Everyone Argues About
stageScoresNote: DRAFT. Stage 1 (Primary Analysis) and Stage 6 (Synthesis) complete; independent verification done. External Stage 2 (Bias Audit) and Stage 3 (Fact Verification) not yet run. Several MOF Fiscal Outlook PDFs returned HTTP 403 to automated retrieval; figures are corroborated across multiple named outlets quoting those documents and must be confirmed against the primary PDFs before publication.
finalScoreNote: Provisional. The core thesis rests on reported actuals (deficit 4.1% and 3.8%, debt 64.7%, the Act 850 benchmarks of 3% and 60%); the debt-path and pension figures labelled as projections are T4A extrapolations on stated assumptions, not observed facts.

figures.title: The visible debt and the part below the waterline
figures.alt: Stacked bar showing federal debt at 64.7% of GDP, total debt and liabilities rising to 84.1%, and a dashed outline to roughly 97% if guarantees are called.
figures.caption: Federal debt is the part above the waterline. Guarantees and committed liabilities lift the total to 84.1% of GDP.
figures.sourceLine: Source: MOF Fiscal Outlook 2026 (end-June 2025); stress figure per MOF guarantee stress test.

figures.title: Three debt numbers, one ceiling
figures.alt: Bar chart of statutory debt 63.5%, total federal debt 64.7%, and debt plus liabilities 84.1%, with the 65% statutory ceiling and 60% target lines.
figures.caption: Only statutory debt is bound by the 65% ceiling. The other measures face no statutory limit.

figures.title: Target beaten, law still missed
figures.alt: Bar chart of the fiscal deficit: 5.0% (2023), 4.1% (2024 actual), 3.8% (2025 target), 3.5% (2026 target), 3.0% (2028), against the 3% legal benchmark.
figures.caption: The 2024 deficit beat its annual target. The Act 850 benchmark of 3% now slips to 2028.

figures.title: Two paths from 64.7%
figures.alt: Line chart of debt to GDP from 2025 to 2035 under two scenarios: consolidation falling to 58.5%, stalled reform rising to 73.7%, against the 65% ceiling.
figures.caption: On a consolidation path debt drifts below the ceiling. On a stalled path it breaches the statutory measure by 2026.
figures.sourceLine: T4A projection using the standard debt-dynamics recursion, effective interest rate 4.2%.

figures.title: The pension bill that answers to no ceiling
figures.alt: Line chart of civil-service pension spending from RM26.4B (2020) to RM32B (2023), projected toward roughly RM98B to RM120B by 2040.
figures.caption: Pension spending roughly triples by 2040, and sits entirely outside the debt ceiling.
figures.sourceLine: Source: Dewan Rakyat answers (2020 to 2023 actuals); T4A projection brackets the official RM120B (2040).

sections.title: The Uncapped Ledger
sections.subtitle: How Malaysia's real fiscal exposure hides outside the number everyone argues about
whatYoullLearn: Why Malaysia has three different debt numbers, and which one the law actually caps
How the country beat its deficit target while still missing its own Fiscal Responsibility Act
Where the RM424.7 billion in guarantees and the trillion-ringgit pension liability sit on the books
Two debt paths to 2035, and the assumptions that decide which one happens

sections.title: The dossier in 7 lines
bullets: Malaysia reports three nested debt figures: statutory debt 63.5%, total federal debt 64.7%, and total debt plus liabilities 84.1% of GDP (end-June 2025).
Only statutory debt is bound by the 65% ceiling. The other two carry no statutory limit.
The 2024 deficit came in at 4.1%, beating the annual target, yet the Fiscal Responsibility Act benchmarks of 3% deficit and 60% debt are both still being missed.
Government guarantees stand at RM424.7 billion (21.1% of GDP). If they were all called, the stress test puts exposure near 97% of GDP.
Civil-service pensions cost RM32 billion in 2023 and are on track toward RM100 billion or more by 2040, none of it counted against the debt ceiling.
The number of working-age adults behind each retiree falls from about 7.9 in 2022 to about 4.6 by 2040.
The fiscal debate fixates on one reassuring figure. The exposure that matters sits about twenty points higher, and growing.

sections.title: The Numbers Behind the Number
sections.subtitle: Why 64% and 84% are both true at the same time

sections.title: Three debt numbers, one ceiling
sections.body:
Ask how indebted Malaysia is and you can get three honest answers. Statutory debt, the measure of Malaysian Government Securities, Government Investment Issues and Treasury bills, stood at 63.5% of GDP at end-June 2025. Total federal government debt, which adds other instruments, was 64.7%. Total debt and liabilities, which adds committed guarantees and public-private partnership obligations, reached 84.1%, about RM1.69 trillion.

The distinction is not pedantry. The 65% ceiling written into law in 2021 binds only the first measure. The second and third face no statutory limit at all. When coverage reports that debt is 64% and still under the ceiling, it quietly swaps the capped number for the uncapped one, and reassures on the wrong basis.

sections.title: Beat the target, missed the law
sections.body:
The 2024 fiscal deficit came in at 4.1% of GDP, inside the 4.3% target the government had set for the year. That was reported, fairly, as a win. The targets for 2025 and 2026 are 3.8% and 3.5%.

The quieter fact: the Public Finance and Fiscal Responsibility Act 2023 sets a medium-term benchmark of a deficit no greater than 3% and debt no greater than 60% of GDP. Both are still being missed. The IMF, in its 2026 Article IV review, endorsed the consolidation path while noting that reaching 3% within five years is not assured. Beating a discretionary annual target and meeting a statutory benchmark are different achievements, and only one of them has happened.
citations: MOF, Malaysia Exceeds GDP and Fiscal Deficit Targets for 2024
MOF Fiscal Outlook 2026, fiscal balance section
IMF Article IV Consultation 2026, Executive Board conclusion
Public Finance and Fiscal Responsibility Act 2023 (Act 850)

sections.body: The headline is not wrong. It is the wrong number. The figure that is legally capped, statutory debt, is the one chosen to look reassuring. The figure that captures real exposure, the all-in 84.1%, is the one no ceiling touches.

sections.title: The Fork
sections.subtitle: Where the trajectory splits, and what bends it

sections.title: Two paths from 64.7%
sections.body:
Debt dynamics are governed by a simple recursion: this year's debt ratio equals last year's, scaled by interest and growth, plus the primary deficit. With an effective interest rate near 4.2% on the federal stock, two assumptions decide everything: how fast nominal GDP grows, and whether the primary deficit keeps shrinking.

Hold to the consolidation glide-path, with nominal growth near 6% and the primary deficit falling toward balance, and the ratio drifts down to about 58.5% by 2035. Let growth slip to 4.5% and the primary deficit stick near 1.1%, and the same starting point climbs to about 73.7%, breaching the statutory measure as early as 2026. The gap between the two lines is not luck. It is policy.

sections.title: What bends the path
sections.body: Three levers move the trajectory. The first is the subsidy bill: the RON95 rationalisation launched in September 2025 was meant to save up to RM8 billion a year, a target since revised down toward RM2.5 to RM4 billion, with the World Bank publicly questioning even that. The second is debt service, already 16.3% of revenue in 2025 and rising toward 17% in 2026. The third is growth itself, the denominator that flatters or punishes every ratio. None of the three is locked in, which is precisely why the fork stays open.
citations: MOF Fiscal Outlook 2026, expenditure and debt service sections
MOF press citation, RON95 subsidy fourth quarter 2025
The Edge, World Bank questions RON95 savings estimate

sections.title: The Liabilities With No Ceiling
sections.subtitle: Guarantees, pensions, and the support base behind them

sections.title: Four hundred billion in guarantees
sections.body: Beyond the debt sits RM424.7 billion in committed government guarantees, about 21.1% of GDP, below the 25% cap that Act 850 does place on guarantees. The largest single exposure is DanaInfra Nasional, followed by Prasarana, the public-sector home-financing body, and the student-loan fund PTPTN. Public-private partnership commitments add a further RM112 billion. The MOF's own stress test is blunt: if every guarantee were called, debt could reach about 97% of GDP. These are contingent, not certain. But they are real, and they move with infrastructure and patronage decisions that rarely reach the fiscal headlines.
citations: MOF Fiscal Outlook 2026, debt and liabilities section
The Star, combined debt and liabilities touch 84.1% of GDP
The Edge, guarantee stress test near 97%

sections.title: The pension bill that answers to no ceiling
sections.body:
Civil-service pension spending rose from RM26.4 billion in 2020 to RM32 billion in 2023, with the number of pensioners climbing toward one million. On an 8.6% compound trend, and on the government's own projections, the bill reaches roughly RM46 billion by 2030 and RM120 billion by 2040. An independent log-linear fit lands in the same place, between RM98 billion and RM130 billion by 2040.

Emoluments and pensions together already absorb about 45% of operating expenditure. The unfunded pension liability, put variously at RM483 billion, RM800 billion, or RM1.3 trillion depending on method, sits entirely off the debt-to-GDP ratio and outside any statutory cap.
citations: Dewan Rakyat answers on pension expenditure and pensioner headcount
MOF Fiscal Outlook 2026, emoluments and retirement charges
The Star, the RM1.3 trillion pension liability problem

sections.title: Fewer shoulders under each retiree
sections.body: The pension promise is paid largely as you go, out of current revenue, so the base of working-age taxpayers matters as much as the bill itself. In 2022 about 7.9 working-age adults stood behind each Malaysian aged 65 and over. On the official cohort-component projection, that falls to about 4.6 by 2040 as the old-age dependency ratio rises from 12.6 to 21.7 per hundred. A rising obligation, met by a shrinking base, is the part of the pension story the annual figures never show.

sections.title: Why none of it is capped
sections.body: The Fiscal Responsibility Act disciplines three things: statutory debt, the headline deficit, and guarantees. It says nothing about total liabilities, nothing about the pension obligation, and nothing about the support base that funds it. The result is a ledger where the most disciplined number is also the least complete, and the largest exposures grow in the space the law does not reach. This is not hidden in the sense of concealed. It is published, in tables one page away from the figure that gets quoted.

sections.claim: Malaysia's debt is 64% of GDP and safely under the 65% ceiling, so the fiscal position is sound.
sections.correction: 64.7% is total federal debt, which the ceiling does not bind; the capped measure is statutory debt at 63.5%. Add guarantees and committed liabilities and the figure is 84.1%, with a stress case near 97%. The ceiling governs the most flattering of three numbers.

sections.source: Public Finance and Fiscal Responsibility Act 2023 (Act 850), fiscal objectives
sections.body: The medium-term fiscal objective is a deficit not exceeding 3% of GDP and federal government debt not exceeding 60% of GDP.
sections.commentary: Both thresholds are written in statute. Both are currently exceeded, with the 3% goal now targeted for 2028, two years past the original window.

sections.title: How the ceiling moved
sections.intro: The statutory debt ceiling is not a constant. It has been raised twice in five years, each time to accommodate the borrowing of the moment.

events.label: Ceiling set at 55%
events.outcome: The statutory limit on debt to GDP is fixed at 55%.

events.label: Raised to 60%
events.outcome: Pandemic borrowing lifts the ceiling to 60% of GDP.

events.label: Raised to 65%
events.outcome: The current 65% statutory ceiling is set.

events.label: Act 850 benchmarks
events.outcome: The Fiscal Responsibility Act sets medium-term goals of 3% deficit and 60% debt.

events.label: Deficit 4.1%
events.outcome: The annual target is beaten while the statutory benchmark is missed.

sections.body: A ceiling that rises to meet the debt is not a constraint on the debt. It is a record of it.

sections.title: What to watch, in order
sections.body: Four signals tell you which path Malaysia is on, well before the annual budget does. Watch debt service as a share of revenue: once it passes a sixth of every ringgit collected, the room to consolidate narrows. Watch the realised subsidy savings against the RM8 billion that was promised. Watch nominal growth, the denominator that does the quiet work. And watch whether the next budget caps anything beyond statutory debt. The headline ratio will be the last thing to move, not the first.

Malaysia's fiscal managers are not reckless, and the consolidation since 2023 is real. The danger is narrative, not arithmetic: a debate anchored on the one number the law happens to cap, while the exposures that carry no ceiling, guarantees, pensions, and a thinning tax base, compound out of view. A government can beat a target it sets itself. It cannot, for long, outgrow the obligations it does not count.
